shots.caption: Everyone at this table is lying
shots.narration: This is Kursi — a bluffing game set in a satirical Indian corporate-political underworld, where everyone at the table is lying about what they hold.

shots.caption: Stamp your seat at the Darbar
shots.narration: You start by stamping your own seat at the Darbar — a name, an avatar, a colour.

shots.caption: Learn to catch a bluff
shots.narration: The tutorial doesn't just explain the rules — it walks you through catching a live bluff, card by card.

shots.caption: Claim a role, dare the room
shots.narration: At the table, every turn is a claim — declare a role, take the action, and dare the room to call you on it.

shots.caption: Block, challenge, replay exactly
shots.narration: Every claim can be blocked or challenged, and underneath it all sits a fully deterministic engine — any match replays byte-for-byte from nothing but a seed and its move log.

shots.caption: The bots' own AI, on your side
shots.narration: Long-press a rival and the same search algorithm driving the ten bot personas — Information Set Monte Carlo Tree Search — builds you a live dossier on what they're probably holding.

shots.caption: Bots gossip, scheme, hold grudges
shots.narration: Above the engine runs Darbar, the social layer — bots gossip, form pacts, and hold grudges across four story arcs on their own separate deterministic clock.

shots.caption: Five rungs to the top
shots.narration: Climb the Tarakki ki Seedhi ladder through five rungs of rising difficulty, or drop into ranked, team play, or a loud ten-player table.

shots.caption: The chair holds one person
shots.narration: Every match ends with a stamped verdict — bluffs held, bluffs caught, decision quality graded against the bot's own best move — because the chair only ever holds one person at a time.
